query: 2010 ACLS guidelines defibrillation and CPR sequence

retrieved_docs.title: Advanced Cardiac Life Support (ACLS) -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The current consensus focuses on early recognition, high-quality CPR, and defibrillation of shockable rhythms. Adequate ACLS delivery relies heavily on basic life support (BLS) principles, with high-quality chest compressions being the cornerstone of neurologically intact survival. High-quality chest compressions are performed at the correct rate and depth, with complete recoil and minimal interruption. The adequate delivery of resuscitation, the education of the lay public and resuscitation providers, and the establishment of a well-functioning chain of survival are critical for improving resuscitation outcomes. [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Advanced Cardiac Life Support (ACLS) -- Issues of Concern -- Monitoring During Cardiac Arrest
retrieved_docs.content: The ACLS guidelines emphasize the importance of clinical and physiological monitoring to improve CPR performance and detect ROSC. Continuous assessment, along with immediate feedback on chest compression rate and depth, chest recoil, and ventilation rate and force, is essential for effective CPR. Devices equipped with accelerometers, either integrated into defibrillator pads or used standalone, provide real-time metrics and feedback during compressions.

retrieved_docs.title: Defibrillation -- Technique or Treatment -- Adult Defibrillation
retrieved_docs.content: According to the ACLS VF/pulseless VT algorithm outlined by AHA, 1 mg of epinephrine should be administered intravenously every 3 to 5 minutes during the resuscitation after the first unsuccessful defibrillation. High-quality CPR is immediately resumed after the second shock for 2 minutes. Suppose VF or pulseless VT persists at the next pulse and rhythm check. In that case, the ACLS algorithm allows 300 mg of amiodarone to be administered intravenously as a bolus during the resuscitation. Suppose additional doses of antiarrhythmic drugs are needed. In that case, 150 mg of amiodarone may be given, or 1 mg/kg to 1.5 mg/kg of lidocaine as a first dose, and 0.5 to 0.75 mg/kg as a second dose may be administered. According to the current AHA guidelines, epinephrine and amiodarone are preferred over lidocaine. [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Advanced Cardiac Life Support (ACLS) -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The American Heart Association (AHA) develops guidelines for CPR that provide evidence-based recommendations on managing cardiovascular emergencies and cardiac arrest. These guidelines date back to 1966 and have been periodically updated by the AHA based on emerging evidence and trials. [4] Notable improvements have been made over the years since the initial CPR guidelines were published. Significant improvements have been observed in outcomes for in-hospital cardiac arrest, whereas advancements in the emergency medical services (EMS) response to out-of-hospital cardiac arrest have lagged behind considerably. [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Advanced Cardiac Life Support (ACLS) -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: The impact of ACLS training on cardiac arrest outcomes is profound. Research shows that healthcare professionals well-trained in ALS protocols perform life-saving interventions more proficiently, leading to improved survival rates and better neurological outcomes. This training equips clinicians with the necessary skills to make rapid, evidence-based decisions during critical situations, which is essential for effective cardiac emergency management. Regular ACLS recertification ensures healthcare professionals remain proficient and up-to-date with evolving best practices and guidelines, thereby enhancing overall care quality. [68]

retrieved_docs.title: Advanced Cardiac Life Support (ACLS) -- Issues of Concern -- Management of Pulseless Rhythms
retrieved_docs.content: Patients experiencing a witnessed cardiac arrest in pulseless ventricular tachycardia or ventricular fibrillation, with pads already applied, can be defibrillated immediately. In contrast, individuals who cannot be defibrillated promptly should receive high-quality CPR until defibrillation is available, ideally as soon as possible. Patients who remain in pulseless ventricular tachycardia or ventricular fibrillation after defibrillation and 1 cycle of CPR should receive 1 mg of IV epinephrine every 3 to 5 minutes while high-quality CPR is ongoing. Amiodarone 300 mg IV or lidocaine 1 to 1.5 mg/kg IV should be administered if pulseless ventricular tachycardia or ventricular fibrillation persists despite defibrillation, epinephrine, and high-quality CPR.

retrieved_docs.title: Advanced Cardiac Life Support (ACLS) -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Approximately 350,000 people in the United States experience out-of-hospital cardiac arrest annually, with 60% to 80% of these individuals not making it to the hospital. [1] Approximately 10.4% of patients who experience cardiac arrest outside the hospital survive their initial hospital admission, with only 8.2% achieving good neurological and functional status. [2] In-hospital cardiac arrest occurs in around 1.2% of adult patients in United States hospitals. Of these patients, 25.8% are discharged alive, and 82% of those who are discharged have good neurological outcomes. The key components for consistently improving patient outcomes, both inside and outside hospitals, include guideline-based management, training in layperson cardiopulmonary resuscitation (CPR), and the design of a chain of survival system. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Advanced Cardiac Life Support (ACLS) -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: The ACLS guidelines developed by the AHA aim to improve the management of severe cardiovascular emergencies, including cardiac arrest, stroke, and acute coronary syndromes. These guidelines encompass advanced medical procedures, medications, and techniques to stabilize patients and improve survival rates. Key components of ACLS include high-quality CPR, arrhythmia management, defibrillation, advanced airway placement, and IV medication administration. The structured algorithms provided in ACLS training enable healthcare professionals to make rapid, evidence-based decisions during critical situations, ensuring each step aims to optimize patient outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: Advanced Cardiac Life Support (ACLS) -- Issues of Concern -- Recognition and Initial Management of Cardiac Arrest
retrieved_docs.content: Previous editions of the ACLS guidelines recommended an Airway-Breathing-Circulation approach to cardiac arrest. This recommendation was revised in 2010, prioritizing circulation as the primary focus in patients experiencing cardiac arrest. The change was made based on resuscitation research demonstrating that early initiation of CPR improved cardiac arrest outcomes compared to early airway management. Lone rescuers or healthcare providers should initiate chest compressions immediately and delay ventilation until additional providers are available to take over. The chest compressions-first strategy has been proven to result in faster initiation of chest compressions, shorter time to ventilation, and earlier completion of the first CPR cycle. [24] [25] [26] [27]

retrieved_docs.title: Asystole -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Asystole is a nonshockable rhythm. Therefore, defibrillation should not be attempted if asystole is noted on the cardiac monitor and confirmed on at least 2 leads. High-quality CPR must continue with minimal (<5 seconds) interruption until ACLS is activated, the patient starts moving, or ROSC is documented. CPR should not stop even for endotracheal intubation.  Advanced life support should be considered after 2 minutes of performing high-quality CPR without ROSC (see Image . Adult Cardiac Arrest Algorithm 2020). The following are the steps in the ACLS guidelines:

retrieved_docs.title: Advanced Cardiac Life Support (ACLS) -- Issues of Concern -- Recognition and Initial Management of Cardiac Arrest
retrieved_docs.content: High-quality CPR and defibrillation are critical lifesaving interventions for cardiac arrest. Resuscitation should occur at the scene where the patient is found if the scene allows. The location should allow for the safe administration of high-quality CPR. Firm surfaces are preferred for CPR, with the patient in the supine position when possible. If the patient cannot be placed in the supine position, prone-position CPR techniques may be used alternatively in the hospital setting. Rescuers should place the heel of their dominant hand in the middle of the chest at the lower half of the sternum, with the heel of the nondominant hand on top of the first in an overlapping position. Metrics for high-quality CPR are constantly being researched and evolving with guideline updates over time.

retrieved_docs.title: EMS Termination Of Resuscitation And Pronouncement of Death -- Clinical Significance -- Resuscitation in Nontraumatic Cardiopulmonary Arrest
retrieved_docs.content: While the current NAEMSP guidelines do not endorse any set time for EMS to perform resuscitation at the scene, the previous NAEMSP guidelines and the current European Resuscitation Council recommend 20 minutes of on-scene efforts before terminating efforts. [25] [26] [25] This recommendation has led to many departments implementing rules for terminating resuscitation, including providing at least 20 minutes of on-scene CPR. [27] Also, EMS agencies must have active physician oversight when making protocols and must consider the providers' training. Below is an example protocol from an active suburban/urban EMS service: CPR may be terminated by ALS and BLS personnel when:

retrieved_docs.title: Advanced Cardiac Life Support (ACLS) -- Issues of Concern -- Termination of Resuscitation Efforts
retrieved_docs.content: The BLS termination-of-resuscitation (TOR) rule recommends terminating resuscitation when 3 criteria are met before transport—the arrest was not witnessed by EMS providers or first responders, ROSC was not achieved, and no shocks were delivered. A meta-analysis of 7 studies involving 33,795 patients found that only 0.13% of patients meeting these criteria survived to hospital discharge.

retrieved_docs.title: Cardiopulmonary Arrest in Adults -- Treatment / Management -- Advanced Cardiac Life Support
retrieved_docs.content: Providers can use basic life support along with advanced airway aid and medication like epinephrine and amiodarone for CPR. The advanced airway may include supraglottic airway devices and endotracheal tubes. ACLS team has the further advantage of cardiac rhythm interpretation and using defibrillation when indicated.

retrieved_docs.title: Advanced Cardiac Life Support (ACLS) -- Function
retrieved_docs.content: The ACLS guidelines aim to improve outcomes in both out-of-hospital and in-hospital cardiac arrest by standardizing cardiac arrest management. The guidelines make cardiac arrest management accessible to prehospital and other healthcare providers based on the best available resuscitation science.

retrieved_docs.title: Advanced Cardiac Life Support (ACLS) -- Issues of Concern -- Termination of Resuscitation Efforts
retrieved_docs.content: The ALS TOR rule similarly advises TOR under specific conditions—the arrest was not witnessed, no bystander CPR was provided, no ROSC was achieved after full ALS care in the field, and no shocks were delivered. A meta-analysis of 2 studies involving 10,178 patients showed a survival rate to hospital discharge of only 0.01% for patients meeting the ALS termination criteria.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 3 -- Community 2282
retrieved_docs.content: Cardiac arrest and ventricular fibrillation are clinical situations in which the use of digoxin is contraindicated, meaning that digoxin should not be administered to patients experiencing these life‑threatening cardiac arrhythmias.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 3 -- Community 2453
retrieved_docs.content: Other nonoperative procedures include the subcategory Other miscellaneous procedures. Other miscellaneous procedures have a broader relationship to إنعاش قلبي رئوي (cardiopulmonary resuscitation), indicating that CPR is considered part of these miscellaneous, non‑operative interventions. In addition, إنعاش قلبي رئوي is classified as a child of the Nonmechanical method of resuscitation, linking it to resuscitation techniques that do not rely on mechanical devices. Consequently, cardiopulmonary resuscitation is both a nonmechanical resuscitation method and an element of the broader group of other miscellaneous procedures within the overall framework of other nonoperative procedures, clarifying its place in procedural taxonomy for clinical documentation and patient care planning.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 4 -- Community 2843
retrieved_docs.content: Cardiac arrest and ventricular fibrillation are clinical situations in which the use of digitoxin is contraindicated; digitoxin should not be administered to patients experiencing these life‑threatening arrhythmias because it can exacerbate cardiac instability.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 2 -- Community 944
retrieved_docs.content: be used to treat تسرع القلب الانتيابي, but it is contraindicated in إِحْصار القلب غير محدد, indicating that while adenosine can terminate certain tachycardias, it should be avoided in patients with unspecified heart block due to the risk of exacerbating conduction failure. Together, these relationships delineate a network in which various rhythm disorders, their anatomical substrates, therapeutic options, and contraindications are interlinked, providing a comprehensive framework for understanding and managing cardiac conduction and rhythm abnormalities.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 3 -- Community 2098
retrieved_docs.content: Therapeutic is identified as the intended purpose of both مكملات الكالسيوم (calcium supplements) and إنعاش قلبي رئوي (cardiopulmonary resuscitation, CPR). In addition, إنعاش قلبي رئوي is classified as a child concept of Nonmechanical method of resuscitation, indicating that CPR is a type of non‑mechanical resuscitation technique. Consequently, calcium supplements and CPR are both therapeutic interventions, with CPR further situated within the broader category of nonmechanical resuscitation methods.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 1524
retrieved_docs.content: Chlorid vápenatý (calcium chloride) can be employed in the treatment of توقف القلب (cardiac arrest), but its administration is contraindicated in the presence of رَجَفانٌ بطيني (ventricular fibrillation) and in patients with فرط كالسيوم الدم (hypercalcemia). Recognizing these therapeutic indications and contraindications is essential for safe patient care and for avoiding adverse outcomes when using chlorid vápenatý.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2566
retrieved_docs.content: Nonmechanical method of resuscitation encompasses manual techniques for reviving patients, and its specific child concept is إنعاش قلبي رئوي (cardiopulmonary resuscitation), which represents the hands‑on approach used to restore breathing and circulation without reliance on mechanical devices.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 3 -- Community 1701
retrieved_docs.content: Respiratory failure; insufficiency; arrest (adult) is a specific adult respiratory condition that is classified under the broader category of اضطراب تَنَفُّسِيّ (respiratory disorder).

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 1572
retrieved_docs.content: Extracorporeal circulation auxiliary to open heart procedures is classified as شلّ القلب, indicating that this supportive technique used during open‑heart surgery is categorized under the concept of cardiac arrest.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 3 -- Community 1507
retrieved_docs.content: Deslanosid is classified as a kardiotonika, a type of cardiotonic agent, and is employed to improve the condition described by انخفاض النتاج القلبي (low cardiac output). However, deslanosid is contraindicated in patients who are experiencing cardiac arrest and ventricular fibrillation, meaning it should not be used in these acute, life‑threatening arrhythmias. Recognizing deslanosid’s role as a cardiotonic drug for low cardiac output while avoiding its use during cardiac arrest or ventricular fibrillation is essential for safe and effective patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 3 -- Community 1614
retrieved_docs.content: Chlorid vápenatý (calcium chloride) is associated with serious cardiac complications, including توقف القلب (cardiac arrest), and its use is contraindicated in the presence of فرط كالسيوم الدم (hyper‑calcemia). Recognizing that calcium chloride can precipitate life‑threatening arrhythmias and that hyper‑calcemia represents a condition in which calcium chloride should not be administered is essential for safe patient management and for preventing adverse outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2750
retrieved_docs.content: Acebutolol, a beta‑blocker, may be used in the treatment of cardiac arrest and ventricular fibrillation, indicating its potential therapeutic role in managing these serious cardiac emergencies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 4 -- Community 2870
retrieved_docs.content: Cardiac dysrhythmias are classified under the broader category تَسَارُّعُ فَوقَ البُطَينِيّ and frequently co‑occur with رفرفة أذينية. Recognizing that cardiac dysrhythmias belong to the supraventricular tachycardia family and are often associated with atrial flutter helps clinicians anticipate related rhythm disturbances, guide diagnostic evaluation, and tailor therapeutic strategies for optimal patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1981
retrieved_docs.content: Magnesium citrate is contraindicated in patients who have an intestinal perforation, as its use in the presence of this condition is not recommended.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 3 -- Community 2436
retrieved_docs.content: Atenolol may be used to treat cardiac arrest and ventricular fibrillation, providing a therapeutic option for these life‑threatening arrhythmias. In contrast, digitoxin is contraindicated in the setting of cardiac arrest and ventricular fibrillation, indicating that this cardiac glycoside should be avoided in patients experiencing these acute rhythm disturbances. Understanding that atenolol can be beneficial while digitoxin is unsafe in this context is important for selecting appropriate pharmacologic interventions during emergency cardiac care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 3 -- Community 1371
retrieved_docs.content: Cardiac arrest and ventricular fibrillation are critical, life‑threatening cardiac emergencies. Administration of dopamine is contraindicated in the setting of cardiac arrest and ventricular fibrillation, indicating that this vasopressor should be avoided when these arrhythmias are present. Conversely, acebutolol, a beta‑blocker, may be used as a therapeutic agent to treat cardiac arrest and ventricular fibrillation, suggesting a potential role for beta‑adrenergic blockade in managing these severe cardiac events.
